source_metadata.title_raw: Jeffrey A. Mitchell - A-18-084 - July 30, 2018 - Sacramento

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 30, 2018

Jeffrey A. Mitchell
Moskovitz Tiedemann & Girard
400 Capitol Mall, 27th Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-084

Dear Mr. Mitchell:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Capitol Area Development Authority ("CADA") and the Capitol Area Community Development Corporation ("CACDC") regarding the conflict of interest code provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Who is the appropriate code reviewing body for CADA and CACDC?

CONCLUSION

The appropriate code reviewing body for both CADA and CACDC is the City of Sacramento because their jurisdiction is wholly within the City.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

You are the General Counsel for CADA and CACDC, a non-profit public benefit corporation created by CADA. You have been asked to seek formal advice concerning which legislative body, the Sacramento City Council or the Sacramento County Board of Supervisors, is the appropriate code reviewing body for CADA and CACDC's conflict of interest code.

___________________________
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-084
Page No. 2

CADA

CADA is a joint powers authority organized under Section 8160, et seq. for the orderly
management and development of the State of California's residential and commercial properties in
the area surrounding the State Capitol. The Authority's members are the State of California and the
City of Sacramento ("City").

Since its creation in 1978, CADA's development efforts have concentrated on the area
surrounding the State Capitol in Downtown Sacramento. CADA is authorized by law to exercise the
powers of a redevelopment agency in a specific project area. The purpose of the creation of CADA
was to revitalize blighted neighborhoods surrounding the State Capitol.

CADA's project area encompasses two areas within Downtown Sacramento. The first area
is bordered (essentially) by "I" Street on the north, "R" Street on the south, 7th Street on the west,
and 17th Street on the east. The second area is bordered (essentially) by "O" Street on the north, "S"
Street on the south, 17th Street on the west, and the railroad right of way in between 19th and 20th
Streets on the east.

Historically, CADA has submitted revisions to its conflict of interest code to the County of
Sacramento ("County"). Recently, CADA has been informed that the County does not believe it is
the appropriate code reviewing body for CADA. CADA has also been told by the City that the City
does not believe it is the appropriate code reviewing body for CADA. CADA seeks formal advice
on which code reviewing body is legally obligated to review revisions to CADA's conflict of
interest code.

CACDC

CACDC was created by action of CADA's Board of Directors in 2014. CACDC is a
California non-profit public benefit corporation and recognized by the Internal Revenue Service as
tax-exempt under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Service Code. You provided that
CACDC qualifies as a local government agency under the criteria set forth in In re Siegel (1977) 3
FPC Ops. 62. The CADA Board of Directors serves as the Board of Directors of the CACDC. The
CACDC's purpose is to assist the City and State in revitalizing the neighborhood around the State
Capitol building by owning, acquiring, developing, financing, assisting, leasing and managing
residential and commercial projects. CACDA also provides amenities associated with the projects,
develops financing and assists with the installation of publicly owned infrastructure projects.
Additionally, it conducts or performs any ancillary or related activity in furtherance of all of the
foregoing.

CACDC seeks formal advice on which code reviewing body, the City or the County, is the
proper code reviewing body for its conflict of interest code.

File No. A-18-084
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87300 provides that every agency shall adopt a conflict of interest code pursuant to
the provisions of the Act.² The code reviewing body is the government agency charged with
reviewing and approving an agency's conflict of interest code. No code is effective unless approved
by the code reviewing body. (Section 87303.) It is the Act's stated policy that conflict of interest
codes are formulated at the most decentralized level possible, with issues of what should be deemed
an "agency" resolved by the code reviewing body. (Section 87301.) Section 82011 details which
agencies are code reviewing bodies. As is pertinent to your questions, the following are the code
reviewing bodies for local government agencies: (1) The Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") for any local government agency with jurisdiction in more than one county (Section
82011(a)); (2) The county board of supervisors for any county agency and any other local
government agency with jurisdiction wholly within the county, other than the board itself, an
agency of the judicial branch or a city agency (Section 82011(b)); and (3), the city council for any
city agency except for the council itself. (Section 82011(c).)

Though CADC and CACDC operate in partnership with the State, their jurisdiction is solely
within the City of Sacramento.³ Therefore, the Sacramento City Council is the is the appropriate
code reviewing for CADC and CACDC's conflict of interest codes. (Section 82011(c).)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

² Agency means any state or local government agency. (Section 82003.)

³ Jurisdiction means the state with respect to a state agency and, with respect to a local government agency, the
region, county, city, district or other geographical area in which it has jurisdiction. Real property shall be deemed to be
"within the jurisdiction" with respect to a local government agency if the property or any part of it is located within or
not more than two miles outside the boundaries of the jurisdiction or within ten miles of any land owned or used by the
local government agency. (Section 82035.)

sections.question: Who is the appropriate code reviewing body for CADA and CACDC?
